annotations.text: The motor weakness restricted to the intrinsic muscles of the left hand could indicate compression of the C8 root or a problem at a higher level in the spinal cord or even the brain.

Presumably, the strength of the muscles innervated by the C5 root, including the deltoid, rhomboid, and biceps muscles, was normal, and no reflexes were noted to be diminished. That combination of findings is against root involvement at the site of the lesion described on radiographic examination and favors spinal-cord compromise at the C4–5 level as an explanation of the hand weakness.

Fractures of vertebral bodies, subluxations of joints, or acute herniations of disk material can all impinge on nerve roots or the spinal cord. The neurologic presentation can be identical to that in this case.

Cervical-disk herniations are clearly demonstrated on MRI scanning and do not cause bone destruction. A fracture with a hematoma can cause a mass compressing the cord, but one would expect to see bone fragments, which were not shown in this case.

Several pathological categories, including traumatic, metabolic, congenital, neoplastic, and inflammatory lesions, deserve mention in the differential diagnosis of spinal-cord dysfunction associated with cervical vertebral lesions.

The demonstration of erosion of two adjacent vertebral-body end plates with narrowing of the intervening disk space has been considered the radiographic hallmark of infection for over half a century. Destruction of the end plates on either side of the C4–5 disk clearly had occurred in this case, but I was initially surprised by the normal signal characteristics in the anterior part of the disk.

Although trauma cannot be the whole answer, it is very likely that the mechanical blow to the neck aggravated a preexisting pathologic process. Some of the soft tissue shown on the MRI scan might have been displaced in such a way as to cause increased pressure on the cord and aggravate the symptoms.

The nature of the spread of infection through a disk space from one end plate to the next has been well studied with the use of microangiographic methods. The blood supply to the end-plate region comes from subperiosteal arteries that encircle the vertebral bodies above and below the equator and send arterioles into the substance of the bone that become end arteries in adults.

Tumor cells typically enter the marrow space of the vertebral body and proliferate there, sometimes destroying bone and at other times causing the production of new sclerotic bone. For most of the common tumors, traversing the avascular disk space is virtually impossible, presumably because the disk will not support angiogenetic activity.

The authors of a prospective study of solitary bone lesions assessed the value of this blood test and concluded, with a degree of confidence approaching 90 percent, that patients with sedimentation rates above 30 mm per hour had metastatic tumors of unknown origin, marrow-cell tumors, or infections.
The presence of both bone destruction and bone formation suggests that the infection involved this site for two to six weeks and did not begin several days before admission.
The weight of the evidence favors pyogenic infection, and the most likely organism is  *S. aureus*

Rapid cervical flexion perhaps resulted in protrusion of an infected mass against the anterior surface of the spinal cord, with spread of purulent material around the posterior longitudinal ligament into the epidural space.
